1_to_2.summary: You're in the early stages of AI adoption — aware of the opportunity but without structured programs or production deployments. The path to Stage 2 is about establishing foundations: governance, a small team, and your first real pilots.

actions.title: Appoint an AI Program Lead or Chief AI Officer
actions.description: Designate a senior owner accountable for the AI adoption roadmap. This person bridges business, technology, and risk — and is your first visible signal of executive commitment.

actions.title: Draft a 12-Month AI Strategy Document
actions.description: Produce a concise strategy document identifying 3–5 priority AI use cases, required capabilities, and success metrics. Secure board or executive committee sign-off.

actions.title: Establish a Basic Model Risk Management Policy
actions.description: Adopt a lightweight MRM framework aligned with SR 11-7. Define what constitutes a 'model', who validates it, and the minimum documentation requirements for any AI/ML system.

actions.title: Run a Data Readiness Assessment
actions.description: Audit your top 3 priority use cases for data availability, quality, and accessibility. Identify the #1 data gap blocking your first AI pilot and assign an owner to close it within 90 days.

actions.title: Hire 2–3 Foundational AI/ML Roles
actions.description: Recruit a data scientist, ML engineer, and AI product manager as your founding AI team. Partner with a staffing firm specializing in fintech/AI talent if needed.

actions.title: Launch Your First AI Pilot in One Business Line
actions.description: Select the highest-ROI, lowest-risk use case (e.g., fraud scoring, document extraction, or churn prediction) and run a time-boxed 90-day pilot with clear success criteria.

actions.title: Create an AI Use Case Intake Process
actions.description: Set up a simple intake form and review process that business units use to submit AI ideas. Prevents ungoverned shadow AI and surfaces hidden opportunities.

actions.title: Begin AI Ethics and Bias Awareness Training
actions.description: Roll out a 2-hour awareness module on responsible AI, algorithmic bias, and regulatory expectations to your data, technology, risk, and compliance teams.

governance.text: Adopt SR 11-7 model risk management guidance
governance.detail: Federal Reserve / OCC model risk guidance — mandatory for bank models. Define model tiers, validation requirements, and model inventory.

governance.text: Register all AI/ML models in a model inventory
governance.detail: Even a spreadsheet is a start. Track model purpose, owner, data sources, last validation date, and business impact tier.

governance.text: Document adverse action processes for any ML credit model
governance.detail: If any ML output can influence a credit decision, you need adverse action notices compliant with ECOA/Reg B.

governance.text: Begin EU AI Act gap assessment
governance.detail: Classify your AI systems by risk tier (unacceptable, high, limited, minimal). High-risk financial AI requires conformity assessments.

governance.text: Establish vendor AI due diligence checklist
governance.detail: Any AI product purchased from a vendor falls under your MRM framework. Require vendors to provide model cards, validation reports, and performance data.

strategy.title: Building AI Strategy & Governance
actions: Benchmark AI activity at peer institutions using public disclosures
Define your AI ambition level: follower, fast follower, or leader
Map AI opportunities to your top 5 strategic priorities
Draft an AI governance charter with clear RACI across business, tech, risk, and compliance
Set up a quarterly AI steering committee with C-suite representation

data.title: Fixing Data Foundations
actions: Prioritize a single data catalog tool (Alation, Collibra, or open source) and start with one domain
Map data lineage for your top 3 pilot use cases
Assign data stewards in each business unit
Assess cloud readiness: identify regulatory blockers to cloud ML workloads
Establish a data quality scorecard with monthly reporting

talent.title: Building the AI Team
actions: Partner with 1–2 universities for AI internship pipelines
Identify 3–5 internal employees with data science interest and offer upskilling via Coursera/DataCamp
Create AI job families with clear leveling and competitive comp bands
Establish a monthly AI community of practice open to all staff
Explore AI Center of Excellence (CoE) model vs. embedded team model

risk.title: Getting Risk & Compliance Ready
actions: Conduct a 2-day SR 11-7 workshop with model risk and compliance teams
Identify your 'Tier 1' high-risk models requiring full independent validation
Subscribe to OCC, Fed, and CFPB AI guidance newsletters
Engage outside counsel to assess ECOA exposure for any planned credit AI
Start documenting model development lifecycle for all new AI projects

2_to_3.summary: You have pilots running and an emerging AI team, but scaling is proving harder than the first use cases. The path to Stage 3 is about MLOps, broader business line adoption, and repeatable governance that can handle many models simultaneously.

actions.title: Build or Buy an MLOps Platform
actions.description: Implement model versioning, experiment tracking, automated retraining pipelines, and model monitoring. Options: Databricks MLflow, SageMaker, Vertex AI, or Weights & Biases.

actions.title: Expand to 3+ Business Lines
actions.description: Identify AI leads in Lending, Fraud, Compliance, and Trading who own their line's AI roadmap. Each line should have at least one production use case by end of year.

actions.title: Establish a Production Model Monitoring Program
actions.description: Implement automated drift detection, performance dashboards, and alert thresholds for all production models. Define clear re-training triggers and escalation procedures.

actions.title: Scale the AI Team to 15–30 People
actions.description: Grow from a seed team to a full AI Center of Excellence. Add specialized roles: MLOps engineers, AI product managers, AI risk analysts, and business-line AI leads.

actions.title: Launch an Internal AI Upskilling Program
actions.description: Deploy a structured AI curriculum for all staff. Tier 1: AI literacy for all employees. Tier 2: AI tools for power users. Tier 3: AI development for technical staff.

actions.title: Standardize the Model Development Lifecycle (MDLC)
actions.description: Document and enforce a standard process for ideation → data → development → validation → deployment → monitoring for all AI projects. Reduces validation bottlenecks.

actions.title: Implement Automated Fair Lending Testing
actions.description: Integrate disparate impact testing and demographic analysis into your model development pipeline. Test all credit models for ECOA compliance before production.

actions.title: Create a Centralized Feature Store
actions.description: Build a shared feature store serving pre-computed, governed features to all model development teams. Reduces duplicated effort and ensures consistent feature definitions.

governance.text: Implement automated SR 11-7 model validation workflows
governance.detail: Systematize validation documentation, challenger models, and performance benchmarking. Target 6-week validation SLA for Tier 2 models.

governance.text: File EU AI Act high-risk system registrations
governance.detail: Credit scoring, fraud detection, and biometrics are likely 'high-risk' under Annex III. Register with EU AI database and complete conformity assessments.
governance.reg: EU AI Act — Article 49 & Annex III

governance.text: Deploy model performance monitoring per SR 11-7 ongoing oversight
governance.detail: Ongoing monitoring is an explicit SR 11-7 requirement. Automate performance reports quarterly; trigger revalidation when thresholds are breached.

governance.text: Complete CFPB UDAAP AI audit readiness review
governance.detail: CFPB has signaled AI as a UDAAP risk area. Review all customer-facing AI decisions for unfair, deceptive, or abusive outcomes.

governance.text: Conduct annual fair lending model exam preparation
governance.detail: Prepare regression analyses, demographic comparisons, and adverse action documentation for examiner review. Engage outside fair lending counsel.

governance.text: Establish AI third-party vendor review program
governance.detail: Annual reviews of all AI vendors including model performance data, SOC 2 reports, and concentration risk assessment.

data.title: Building MLOps & Data Infrastructure
actions: Evaluate and select a cloud ML platform (AWS SageMaker, GCP Vertex, Azure ML, or Databricks)
Implement Git-based model versioning and experiment tracking
Build automated retraining pipelines triggered by data drift or calendar schedules
Create a real-time feature serving layer for low-latency inference use cases
Establish data contracts between data producers and ML consumers

usecases.title: Scaling Use Cases Across Lines
actions: Run an AI opportunity workshop in each business line to identify top 3 use cases
Create a portfolio view: track all AI initiatives by stage, ROI, and risk
Establish an AI project prioritization framework (impact × feasibility × risk)
Pilot 2 use cases using LLMs / generative AI in lower-risk domains
Measure and publish AI ROI quarterly to build internal momentum

strategy.title: Formalizing Governance at Scale
actions: Upgrade AI governance charter to cover generative AI and LLM-specific risks
Create a Model Risk Committee that meets monthly with CISO, CRO, and CTO
Define a tiered model risk classification system (Tier 1-3) with proportionate oversight
Publish an annual AI transparency report for external stakeholders
Benchmark your governance maturity against NIST AI RMF framework

risk.title: Strengthening Risk & Compliance
actions: Implement real-time bias and fairness dashboards for credit AI
Conduct a red-team adversarial testing exercise on your top 3 production models
Map all production AI to EU AI Act risk tiers and document conformity
File for any required regulatory approvals before expanding AI credit decisioning
Establish a model incident response plan for AI failures or adverse outcomes

3_to_4.summary: You have strong AI foundations and multiple production deployments. The final leap to Stage 4 means AI is woven into your competitive strategy, driving autonomous decisions, and generating proprietary IP. This is about culture, innovation leadership, and AI-native product design.

actions.title: Launch an AI-First Product Strategy
actions.description: Embed AI-native features into your core product roadmap — not as add-ons, but as primary value drivers. Define what a truly AI-native banking product looks like for your customers.

actions.title: Deploy Autonomous AI Decision Systems
actions.description: Move selected use cases to full straight-through AI processing without human review (e.g., small business credit, fraud blocking). Implement robust human-in-the-loop escalation for edge cases.

actions.title: Build Proprietary Foundation Models or Fine-Tune LLMs
actions.description: Invest in fine-tuning large language models on your proprietary data (loan books, customer interactions, transaction patterns) to create differentiated AI capabilities.

actions.title: Establish an AI Research Function or Lab
actions.description: Create a dedicated AI research team publishing papers, filing patents, and contributing to industry standards bodies. Signals innovation leadership and attracts top talent.

actions.title: Implement AI-to-AI Orchestration
actions.description: Deploy multi-agent AI systems where specialized models (credit, fraud, compliance, market risk) work in concert, sharing signals and automating cross-functional workflows.

actions.title: Achieve Board-Level AI Accountability
actions.description: Establish board committee oversight of AI strategy and risk. Publish annual AI transparency report. Appoint a Chief AI Officer with explicit P&L accountability.

actions.title: Monetize AI Capabilities Externally
actions.description: Explore BaaS/API revenue from AI capabilities: fraud scoring, credit decisioning, AML analytics. Partner with fintechs to distribute AI-powered financial products.

actions.title: Join and Lead Industry AI Consortia
actions.description: Take leadership roles in FINOS, Partnership on AI, BIS Innovation Hub, or similar. Sets standards, builds brand, and provides early access to regulatory guidance.

governance.text: Implement autonomous AI decision audit trails
governance.detail: Every fully automated AI decision must be logged with explainable factors, alternative actions considered, and outcome tracking. Required for examiner review.
governance.reg: CFPB / ECOA / EU AI Act Art. 14

governance.text: Complete EU AI Act conformity assessments for all high-risk systems
governance.detail: Full technical documentation, conformity assessment, and EU AI database registration for all Annex III systems by compliance deadline.

governance.text: Establish AI systemic risk reporting to regulators
governance.detail: Large institutions: engage Fed/OCC on AI systemic risk implications. Consider proactive engagement rather than waiting for examination.

governance.text: File AI-related patent applications
governance.detail: Protect proprietary model architectures, feature engineering methods, and novel AI applications through patent filings.

governance.text: Conduct annual AI systemic risk self-assessment
governance.detail: Assess concentration risk from AI vendor dependencies, model monoculture risk, and correlated AI failures across the industry.

governance.text: Publish voluntary AI transparency report
governance.detail: Following NIST AI RMF and Partnership on AI standards, publish externally facing report on AI use, governance, and impact.

usecases.title: Deploying Autonomous AI
actions: Map the human review steps in your top 5 AI workflows and identify which can be safely automated
Build robust exception handling and human escalation for edge cases before removing human review
Define 'confidence thresholds' below which AI defers to human judgment
Run shadow mode testing (AI decision vs. human decision) for 90 days before going autonomous
Establish a real-time override dashboard for human operators monitoring autonomous systems

data.title: Building Proprietary AI Assets
actions: Inventory your unique data assets that could be used to fine-tune LLMs
Evaluate LoRA/QLoRA fine-tuning vs. RAG vs. full pre-training for your use cases
Build a private model hub for internally developed and fine-tuned models
Establish a synthetic data factory for privacy-preserving training data generation
File IP protection for novel model architectures and training methodologies

talent.title: AI-First Culture & Leadership
actions: Add 'AI literacy' as a competency in all employee performance reviews
Create an AI Fellows program: rotating senior engineers into AI research for 6-12 months
Partner with 3+ top-tier universities for AI research collaboration
Sponsor AI hackathons open to all employees — not just technologists
Publish employee-authored AI research papers and blog posts externally

strategy.title: AI-Native Strategy & IP
actions: Rewrite your 3-year strategic plan with AI as the primary growth driver
Identify 2–3 new revenue streams enabled only by your AI capabilities
Explore licensing your AI platform to peer banks or fintechs
Participate in regulatory sandboxes for novel AI financial applications
Develop AI-first partnership strategy with cloud hyperscalers and AI labs

4_maintain.summary: You're an AI-first financial institution. The imperative now is sustained leadership: staying ahead of the frontier, managing systemic AI risk, and shaping industry standards. Your playbook focuses on innovation velocity and responsible scale.

actions.title: Continuous Frontier Model Evaluation
actions.description: Run quarterly bake-offs between your proprietary models and the latest foundation models. Adopt or integrate frontier capabilities faster than competitors.

actions.title: AI Regulatory Leadership Engagement
actions.description: Proactively engage Fed, OCC, CFPB, and EU regulators with your AI governance practices. Shape guidance rather than react to it. Comment on all relevant AI rulemaking.

actions.title: Publish Annual AI Transparency Report
actions.description: Release a comprehensive public report on AI use, model performance, fairness metrics, and governance practices. Sets industry standard and builds stakeholder trust.

actions.title: Invest in AI Safety & Alignment Research
actions.description: Fund or participate in research on AI alignment, interpretability, and safety — especially for autonomous financial AI. Positions you as a responsible AI leader.

actions.title: Build AI Talent Pipeline Programs
actions.description: Establish university partnerships, PhD fellowship programs, and AI apprenticeships. Ensure a 3–5 year talent pipeline to sustain leadership.

actions.title: Explore Quantum-AI Hybrid Strategies
actions.description: Begin quantum computing exploratory programs for portfolio optimization, cryptography migration, and future ML acceleration. 3–5 year horizon investment.

actions.title: Monetize AI Platform as a Service
actions.description: Package your AI infrastructure, models, and governance frameworks as financial AI APIs for community banks, credit unions, and fintechs. Creates a new revenue stream.

actions.title: AI Ethics & Impact Annual Review
actions.description: Conduct a formal review of AI societal impact: job displacement, financial inclusion, algorithmic bias, and environmental footprint of AI compute.

governance.text: Annual AI systemic risk assessment and regulator briefing
governance.detail: Proactively brief primary regulators on AI exposure, concentration risk, and novel use cases. Demonstrates leadership and builds examiner trust.

governance.text: Maintain EU AI Act compliance for all registered high-risk systems
governance.detail: Annual re-assessment, updated technical documentation, and post-market monitoring reports for all Annex III AI systems.

governance.text: NIST AI RMF annual maturity assessment
governance.detail: Formal gap assessment against NIST AI RMF 1.0 (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage). Publish results in transparency report.

governance.text: Patent portfolio annual review and filing
governance.detail: Review AI patent portfolio, identify new inventions from the prior year, and file continuation patents for maturing innovations.

strategy.title: Sustaining AI Leadership
actions: Establish a 5-year AI technology horizon scanning function
Create an AI Leadership Council with external advisors from academia, policy, and industry
Define 'moonshot' AI projects with 3+ year timelines and dedicated R&D budget
Measure AI competitive advantage through external benchmarking annually
Share AI governance best practices publicly — leadership builds ecosystem

risk.title: Managing Frontier AI Risk
actions: Model systemic risk scenarios: what happens if your AI fails simultaneously across multiple business lines?
Conduct adversarial red-team exercises on your most critical autonomous AI systems
Implement AI circuit breakers — automatic shutdown triggers if anomalous behavior is detected
Engage with BIS, FSB, and IOSCO on international AI financial risk standards
Participate in industry-wide AI incident response frameworks (e.g., CERT for financial AI)

data.title: Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
actions: Evaluate quantum-classical hybrid approaches for portfolio optimization and cryptography
Implement federated learning for privacy-preserving AI across subsidiaries or consortia
Build a real-time AI observability platform spanning all models, features, and outcomes
Explore neuromorphic computing or specialized AI chips for inference efficiency
Create a carbon-aware AI compute strategy to manage the environmental footprint

talent.title: Sustaining AI Talent Excellence
actions: Benchmark AI compensation against Big Tech and hedge funds annually
Create internal AI career paths from analyst to principal researcher
Establish sabbatical program for senior AI staff at partner universities or research labs
Sponsor diversity-focused AI scholarship programs at target universities
Build an alumni network of former AI staff who remain brand advocates and referrers
